chapters.title: Changing your mind is not an act of betrayal
chapters.text:
Most of us think based on assumptions, habits, and instincts that have formed our sense of self. Questioning these beliefs is difficult for many because we are shedding a part of our identities. But the development path requires we amend our mental constitutions to allow open-mindedness.

Cognitive Laziness causes the first instinct fallacy. It is a phenomenon when one gets an answer wrong on the first attempt. Out of 1500 students tested in Illinois, 50% changed their answers from wrong to right, while only 25% changed from right to wrong. This result demonstrates that p eople who rethink their solutions tend to improve their scores.

Changing your mind is okay; never rethinking your views and ideas is much worse.

Our desire for predictability makes us resist rethinking. To prevent our identities from being threatened, we avoid reconsidering what we deeply believe because it makes us feel as if something fundamental in us cracks.

We embrace rethinking when it concerns our possessions. We buy new clothes, renovate our houses, and update our cell phones. Why can't we apply the same principle to our thought patterns? We won't use Windows 95 but will stick to ideas we formed in 1995.

Like a frog in lukewarm water that is gradually heated, we often fail to rethink situations until it is too late. Guess what? The frog story is in valid, but we believe it to be true. A frog dropped in hot water will not leap out of it because it will be badly burned immediately but it can escape lukewarm water when it gets uncomfortable. So, it is not the frog that cannot rethink. We are the ones who often fail to question these stories.

Got it? Stay tuned to uncover more cognitive tricks!

chapters.title: Truth seekers aim for hypothesis refutation, not confirmation
chapters.text:
Psychologist Phil Tetlock found that we slip into three major modes as we think and talk:

• Preacher mode (when expressing our core beliefs)
• Prosecutor mode (when examining other people's opinions)
• Politician mode (when we want to promote ideas that we support)

But there is a fourth mode we all need to engage in to become rethinkers. It is the scientist mode.

Being a scientist means possessing a specific skill set and a unique frame of mind. Fortunately, the scientist mode of thinking is not reserved for the laboratory alone. Anybody can learn to think this way.

There was an experiment with over 100 Italian business founders that cut across different domains. The researchers divided them into two groups. One group received training in the scientific mode of thinking, while the other group did not. The scientific group pivoted numerously and earned 40 times more than the second group, which stuck to the “normal” way of doing business.

However, sticking to one way of thinking requires serious self-control. Sometimes even professional scientists need help to do this. For example, they enter preacher mode to promote their pet theories. Also, occasionally they abandon accuracy for what is popular (politician mode) and can be hellbent on debunking ideas that disagree with theirs (prosecutor mode). Surprisingly, people with high IQs have more difficulty updating their beliefs. Once, math whizzes who were either liberal or conservative were told to analyze data on ideological issues that activate strong emotions. Each group failed to see evidence that contradicted their existing beliefs.

When people think in scientific mode, they do not allow ideas to become ideologies. They teach from evidence rather than preach from intuition. They lead with quizzes and puzzles instead of answers and solutions.

Scientific thinking requires an active search for evidence that might prove us wrong.

Reacting with an open mind is not enough; we must be open-minded by default. Rethinking in preacher mode is a moral weakness. We shift based on sharper logic and more actual data in scientist mode.

The purpose of learning isn't to affirm our beliefs; it's to evolve our beliefs. ~ Adam Grant

Did you know? As of 2009, BlackBerry accounted for almost 50% of the US smartphone market. BlackBerry's intelligent device creator struggled with rethinking, spelling his phenomenal invention's extinction.

chapters.title: The most brilliant minds tend to rethink their ideas
chapters.text:
When you look back at the last year, do you wonder at the foolishness of your past mistakes ? If yes, it's a good thing. It shows you've learned and made progress.

Admitting you were wrong shows that you are honest and willing to grow. Plus, it makes you look more confident. So, detach your present from your past and your opinions from your identity.

When presented with new information, you can stubbornly continue to preach and prosecute or operate like scientists dedicated to pursuing truth rather than sticking to their guns. Real wisdom lies in recognizing your cognitive blindspots and revising your thoughts accordingly.

Apple's success story is a good example. For more than 30 years, the corporation has been creating computer equipment until it released the first iPhone in 2007. Despite Steven Jobs' dislike for smartphones, he embraced intellectual humility when his engineers convinced him to start working in a new field . The iPhone was not his idea, but he was willing to run with the innovative concept, which, as a result, fundamentally improved the company's profit.

Phrases that Steven Jobs might have said, but luckily he didn't (and you shouldn't):

• “That will never work here!”
• “That's not what my experience has shown.”
• “That's too complicated; let's not overthink it.”
• “That's the way we've always done it.”

If you don't change your mind frequently, you're going to be wrong a lot. ~ Jeff Bezos

Instead, Jobs produced a Rethinking Cycle that included:

• Humility
• Doubt
• Curiosity
• Discovery

As you can see, people with flexible minds listen to others' opinions, even if they are already successful in their field.

Those ready to reflect critically on their decisions will always make insights and discoveries.

Sometimes overconfidence drives us into a trap. Failing to accept that one may be wrong or ignorant prevents people from developing. Armchair quarterback syndrome occurs when a person's confidence exceeds their competence level. Most sports fans fall into this category.

The opposite extreme of this phenomenon is impostor syndrome . It occurs when a person's competence outweighs their confidence.

It has at least three upsides; it motivates us:

• To work harder
• To be more thoughtful
• To become better learners

Like everywhere else, it's about the middle. A blend of confidence and humility puts us in the rethinking loop.

Did you know? The ability to think about our thinking is called a metacognitive skill.

chapters.title: Use disagreements to reach the best outcome
chapters.text:
There are two ways to argue. Only one is productive, and we should use it in work. It is a task conflict, which means a clash of different professional views and opinions. In this case, the dispute aims to solve the problem, not to insult one of you. It helps achieve more significant results.

The opposite is a relationship conflict. This way of arguing is always overly emotional, often hostile, and ineffective. It is the surest way to quarrel without completing the assignment.

Relationship conflicts prevent us from rethinking. Task conflicts force us to reconsider our ideas as diverse thoughts are on the table. These ideas make us enter scientist mode, searching for the truth.

Sadly, many of us have developed an aversion to productive disagreements. Yet it is what makes us more civilized and helps us acquire inventive powers.

It is almost impossible to realize the need to rethink your ideas in a resigned team without initiative. Another thing is people who can openly, honestly, and directly point out shortcomings. A focus group that is not afraid to ask clarifying questions and give feedback is the best cure for stagnant non-progressive beliefs.

An example is when Pixar recruited an outside director, Brad Bird, to keep the company firing on all cylinders. Brad had to assemble the team at his discretion, and he chose the most intractable, picky, and contradictory people. Tirelessly correcting each other, they created the Incredibles cartoon in four years. It won an Oscar, made record-breaking profits, and was the most complex animated film in the company's history.

We must prevent task conflicts from spilling into relationship conflicts. How? Treat disputes as discussions. Their main goal is to reveal your point of view to the interlocutor and to understand their opinion. Show that you are interested in having your colleague change your mind. Remember that both of you are trying to find the best solution to the problem, not to be correct.

When you're about to start a task conflict, ask the other person: “Can we debate?” It changes their perception of what is about to ensue. When you ask people how their ideas would work in practice, they are more likely to pick holes in their argument and become more receptive to alternative options.

We debate ideas, not emotions. So, argue about how, not why.

chapters.title: Debate with curiosity and respect to convince your opponents
chapters.text:
People shut down entirely or get riled up when we engage them as adversaries. Instead of opening up, they enter defense, preaching, prosecuting, or politicking modes. Our goal should be to get them to think like scientists. Preaching their perspectives makes them double down on their convictions. Prosecuting ours makes them blind to holes in theirs. Playing politics by telling us what they think we want to know does not allow us to discover what they really believe. A great debate is not a war. It consists of finding common grounds more than looking for attack weapons. Expert negotiators present fewer points, while average debaters think the more reasons they offer , the bigger their chances of winning. Consequently, they run the risk of diluting strong arguments with weak ones. Another feature of skilled negotiators is that they express curiosity more than engaging in an offense-defense strategy. Expert debaters are less assertive. Instead, they occasionally end their comments with questions.

The next time you're in an argument, use this tactic:

• Focus on a small number of core strengths
• Show curiosity that may prompt your audience to rethink their positions
• Have the motivation and ability to learn
• Be guided by logic, facts, and statistics

Our motives must be pure when we ask people to think again. That motivation must be a desire for the truth.

If we want to change other people's minds, we must also be willing to change ours. A genuinely mature person is not afraid to admit their critics were right. The ability to sincerely thank others for pointing out your mistakes, and to take notes into account, indicates good self-esteem and a regulated sense of pride.

By the way, employers highly value this quality. A typical interview question is: “What are your weaknesses?” Also, job interviewers often ask the interviewee about their mistakes and blunders in past jobs. Those who answer honestly and extensively are more likely to get a job than those who hide that information. The secret is that such situations demonstrate if you can think critically, analyze your experience and draw conclusions from it. Feel free to talk about what you have learned from a previous setback. It indicates that you have rethought your mistakes.

chapters.title: Thinking again must happen from the inside out
chapters.text:
Each of your beliefs has a source, reason, and origin. Often, to rethink something that seems unshakable and unconditional, it is necessary to imagine what you would think about it if you were a different person. Suppose you were of another generation, gender, race, or profession; you would look at things differently. Sometimes, you must turn to fantasy to encourage reinterpretation.

For example, try the motivational interview method. Listen carefully to your interlocutor, ask clarifying questions, and then try to retell the essence of their idea. Enquire how they formed their opinion and what experience preceded this. Invite them to do the same with your concept. This way, you can look at the subject of your dispute from the outside. It helps to notice nuances and inconsistencies you previously ignored. As an interviewer, you must ensure that the other person feels like an exciting guest on a show. For now, you need to focus on making them look good, not you. Such interaction with the opponent allows you to avoid overconfidence.

Being overly self-righteous can be a trap that hinders progress.

It is helpful to realize that there can be many different answers to one question. They may or may not be equivalent in their validity. Moreover, some solutions may cease to be accurate or relevant over time. It is how open systems work. They constantly interact with their environment and change under its influence. Examples of such systems are relationships or careers. They operate by two fundamental principles:

• Equifinality, which means multiple paths to the same end.
• Multifinality implies that the same starting point can lead to many different endings.

Consider the instance of a child born into a family of musicians who follows in her parent's footsteps, becoming a composer, but her twin does not. Yet simultaneously, both become successful in different areas. This example explains why people should not rely on only one scenario. We must instill this in our children to make them more flexible and open-minded. Such a skill will benefit them both in education and their personal lives. And the best method to pass something on to future generations is to show it by example. Change starts with working on yourself.

Find enough moral strength to reconsider your past actions and question old decisions. Look at life with curiosity to reassess your current reality. It may lead to a change in your plans for the future.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
It would be best if you accustomed yourself to regular rethinking to keep up with the times. It can be difficult because the reassessment of values ​​deprives you of the usual moral support. However, this is the only way to keep the mind flexible throughout life.

Critical thinking allows us to develop professionally and improve our human qualities. You must stop being afraid of mistakes and let yourself be wrong. Look at your past misconceptions as opportunities to improve. The main thing is to take lessons from experience.

Society should strive to improve its skills in arguing. There should be no aggression or personal hostility in the discussion. We must cultivate respect for the interlocutor and a sincere interest in their ideas, even if they contradict ours.

The world is not black and white. Learning to distinguish shades in it will make us more approachable and happier. And we need this way of thinking in today's world, where everything changes daily. Therefore, we should explain to children early on that it is normal to change their minds. And the primary condition for this is the presence of a deliberate justification and not emotion.

Try this


• Have a mock debate with friends. For example, discuss an idea in the living room after Saturday dinner. Let each of you argue your position while the others ask supporting questions.
• Show your children that there is no shame in not knowing something. Encourage their questions, and don't punish them for their delusions. Instead of making them feel guilty about disagreeing with you, use the “Let's figure it out together” formula.
• When making crucial decisions, give yourself extra time to think. For example, revisit this thought in a day or a week to re-evaluate it.
